"reminiscences of old time gro
Gerry days in Spokane were gone
over at the last meeting of the
Spokane Grocers' association.


Secretary E. N. Cory was a clerk
in the first grocery store ever
started in old Spokane Falls, and
he told of the difficulty with which
the eastern emigrants were recon-
ciled to high prices. There is con-
siderable of the same difficulty ex-
listing still The freight on the
first carload of kerosene, he said,
amounted to 500 from Chicago.
The freight on vinegar came to
about 35 cents on the gallon, con-
sequently these commodities were
retailed in Spokane for rs cents
a gallon. while in the eastern
states they could be purchased for
from IA to 20 cents. Mr.. Cory
stated that the first carload of
kerosene cost but $500 wholesale
in Chicago, making the freight
amount to $110 more than the pur-
chase price.


The meeting was open to the
wives of the members, and with
A. ]. Argall as coffee maker and
Messrs. Crandall, Steffer and Sill
baugh for hashers, the oyster sup
pers was greatly enjoyed.


Mrs. S. B. Crandall, E. M. Cory,
President Bower, A. L. Clausen, M.
A. Doyle and S. A. Bishop were
among the speakers, while Presi-
dent A. W. Bower was POSTMASTER.


1t is reported that although the
bad weather stood against the
dance given by the grocers Janu-
airy IA, the affair was a success,
both financially and socially.
